visualizations.title: Simplex Three-Phase Voting FSM
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand how a consensus round transitions through Propose → Notarize → Nullify → Finalize and what conditions drive each transition.
visualizations.why_it_matters: The phase gating is the core protocol insight. Students frequently confuse 'quorum reached' with 'all votes collected' — the quorum threshold line makes the N3f1 >2/3 requirement concrete and visible.
visualizations.default_state: View=1, Epoch=0, Leader=Validator_0, Phase=Propose, N=4, f=1 (one Byzantine validator). No votes recorded.

scenes.caption: Leader broadcasts a proposal for the current view. The proposal carries round (Epoch, View), leader identity, and parent (previous View, parent digest). Validators receive it and check parent continuity.
visual_elements: Leader node (highlighted)
Proposal message traveling to all validators
Round label: (epoch=0, view=1)
Parent field shown on proposal

scenes.caption: Each honest validator runs Automaton::verify. If valid, they sign and broadcast a Notarize vote. Votes accumulate in VoteTracker. The quorum threshold line (>2N/3) is visible throughout.
visual_elements: Validator nodes with individual vote bars
Notarize vote arrows flowing to certificate assembly
Horizontal quorum threshold line (red dashed): N3f1::quorum(N)
Vote count label: '3/4 notarizes — quorum reached'
AttributableMap state: each validator's notarize slot filled or empty

scenes.caption: When >2N/3 Notarize votes are collected, the notarization certificate forms. The certificate is gossiped. Then CertifiableAutomaton::certify is invoked — this is the deferred verification hook. In the normal path, certify returns true.
visual_elements: Certificate icon assembling from signatures
Certificate being multicasted (broadcast to all)
certify() call shown as a side-channel to Automaton
Phase label transitions: Notarize → Finalize

scenes.caption: Validators who received the notarization certificate and passed certification sign Finalize votes. Finalize votes accumulate. Once >2N/3 finalize votes are collected, the finalization certificate is formed and the block is committed at this height.
visual_elements: Finalize vote bars accumulating
Quorum threshold crossed animation
Finalization certificate icon
Block committed indicator with height
Phase label: Finalize → (next round)

scenes.caption: Leader fails to propose, or proposes an invalid block. After leader_timeout, validators send Nullify votes. When >2N/3 nullify, the view is skipped and the next leader takes over.
visual_elements: Timeout clock icon appearing at each validator
Nullify vote arrows (different color from Notarize)
Quorum threshold line for nullify votes
View counter: 1 → 2
New leader highlighted

scenes.caption: Two competing proposals arrive at the same view. One gathers a quorum of notarizes; the other times out. View change mechanism selects the next leader, who proposes on top of the notarized chain.
system_events: proposal_A gains notarizes from validator_0, validator_1, validator_2
view changes to 2, new leader proposes on top of A's chain
visual_elements: Two proposal paths diverging from leader
Quorum reached on path A (green highlight)
Timeout on path B (red X)
Chain fork visualization
Arrow showing view change: leader_B proposes on A's chain

checks: Notarize vote requires Automaton::verify to return true
Finalize vote requires prior Notarize certificate (no orphaned finalization)
Quorum threshold = N3f1::quorum(N) = ceil(N - N/3)
Each validator can cast at most one vote per phase (AttributableMap enforces this)
After finalization, next round begins with the finalized block as parent

visualizations.title: Leader Election: RoundRobin vs Random
visualizations.learning_goal: Compare deterministic round-robin rotation with VRF-based random leader selection, and understand why Random falls back to round-robin for view 1.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Leader election is subtle: Random's VRF depends on a prior certificate for seed derivation, which doesn't exist at view 1. The fallback is intentional and necessary — without it, there would be no deterministic leader for the first view.
visualizations.default_state: N=7 validators, epoch=0, view=1. No certificates yet. Random elector falls back to round-robin for this view.

scenes.caption: RoundRobin elects leader as (epoch + view) % N. At view 1 with 7 validators, leader index = (0 + 1) % 7 = 1. No certificate dependency — rotation is fully deterministic.
visual_elements: Validator ring showing all 7 participants
Highlighted leader node at index 1
Formula displayed: leader = (epoch + view) % N = (0 + 1) % 7 = 1
No certificate input arrow (certificate=None)

scenes.caption: Random elector at view 1 has no certificate to seed the VRF, so it falls back to round-robin. The fallback is intentional — VRF output is pseudorandom but requires a seed; no prior certificate means no seed.
visual_elements: VRF box showing 'NO CERTIFICATE — FALLBACK'
Arrow pointing to round-robin formula
Same leader index 1 as RoundRobin for comparison

scenes.caption: For view 2, Random uses the Finalize certificate from view 1 as the VRF seed. The BLS threshold signature over the finalized block becomes the input to the VRF. The VRF output is a pseudorandom index derived via modulo arithmetic.
visual_elements: Certificate from view 1 flowing into VRF box as seed
VRF computation visualization
Output modulo N producing leader index
Leader index potentially different from round-robin
Side-by-side comparison: RoundRobin → 2, Random → 4

scenes.caption: After 50 rounds, RoundRobin shows a perfectly regular rotation pattern. Random shows an unpredictable sequence with no detectable pattern. The two sequences diverge significantly after the first few views.
system_events: elect() called for views 1..50 with appropriate certificates
visual_elements: Two vertical leader sequence columns (RoundRobin | Random)
Each row = one view
Color-coded participant indices (0–6)
Divergence point marked where Random deviates from RoundRobin
Pattern regularity indicator: RoundRobin shows periodic peaks, Random shows white noise

checks: elect() is deterministic: same round + same certificate → same leader index
View 1 with certificate=None always falls back to round-robin for Random
Random elector requires a certificate for views > 1
RoundRobin leader = (epoch + view) % num_participants (or shuffled permutation)

visualizations.title: VoteTracker: Quorum Accumulation in Real Time
visualizations.learning_goal: Watch notarize, nullify, and finalize votes accumulate per validator and observe exactly when the N3f1 quorum threshold is crossed.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Students often think 'quorum reached' means 'all votes collected.' The N3f1 threshold (ceiling of N - N/3) is strictly less than N, meaning finalization requires only a supermajority, not unanimity. Visualizing this distinction is foundational to understanding Byzantine consensus.
visualizations.default_state: N=7, f=1. Quorum threshold = N3f1::quorum(7) = 5. No votes cast yet. Scheme = ed25519 (attributable).

scenes.caption: VoteTracker starts empty. Each validator has one slot per phase. AttributableMap<Notarize> is keyed by validator index. Slots are empty.
visual_elements: Grid: rows = validators (0–6), columns = phases
Notarize column highlighted
All slots show empty state (dashed circle)
Quorum threshold line at right: 'need 5 of 7'

scenes.caption: Votes arrive sequentially. Each insert into AttributableMap either succeeds (slot filled) or returns false (duplicate vote rejected). The bar chart shows vote count approaching quorum.
system_events: validator_0 notarize arrives again → insert fails (duplicate)
visual_elements: Validator 0 bar fills to 1
Validator 1 bar fills to 1
Validator 3 bar fills to 1
Accumulation count: '3/7 notarizes'
Quorum line: '5 needed' (dashed red)
Duplicate vote shown as red flash on validator_0 with 'duplicate rejected' tooltip

scenes.caption: At 5 notarizes (quorum), the threshold is crossed. The system emits a notarization certificate. The bar chart highlights the crossing moment. Remaining validators' bars stay gray — they don't need to vote.
visual_elements: Bar chart with threshold line crossed (green)
Certificate assembly animation
Count label: '5/7 notarizes — QUORUM'
Validators 5 and 6 shown as non-contributing (grayed out)

scenes.caption: Switch to bls12381_threshold. Now each validator's individual signature is not shown — only the aggregate threshold signature appears once quorum is reached. This is non-attribution: no observer can tell which specific validators signed.
system_events: Votes still accumulate internally but display is aggregate only
visual_elements: Per-validator bars replaced with single aggregate bar
Caption: 'Non-attributable: individual signatures not shown'
Tooltip: 'Any 5 partial signatures can forge any other validator's signature'
Certificate shows only one combined signature, not per-signer pairs

checks: N3f1::quorum(N) = ceiling(N - N/3). For N=7, quorum=5. For N=4, quorum=3.
AttributableMap insert returns false on duplicate — one vote per phase per validator
Attributable schemes (ed25519) show per-signer signatures in certificate
Non-attributable schemes (bls12381_threshold) show only recovered threshold signature
Quorum reached does NOT mean all N validators voted — only >2N/3

visualizations.title: Ordered Broadcast: Sequencer Chains and Validator Quorums
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand how sequencers produce chained nodes where each node certifies the previous, making equivocation detectable and attributable.
visualizations.why_it_matters: The chaining mechanism is the key design choice: each chunk includes a certificate over the previous chunk, so an honest sequencer's misbehavior (equivocating at the same height) produces detectable conflicting signatures from the same validator.
visualizations.default_state: Sequencer=Validator_0, N=4 validators, epoch=0. Chain height=0 (genesis node with parent=None).

scenes.caption: Genesis node at height=0 has parent=None. The sequencer broadcasts it; validators sign and form a quorum certificate. This certificate is stored and referenced as the parent for height=1.
system_events: Certificate stored as parent for next node
visual_elements: Node box at height 0: 'chunk_0, parent=None'
Validator signatures flowing to certificate assembly
Certificate box (diamond shape) labeled 'cert_0'
Arrow: cert_0 → used as parent for height 1

scenes.caption: Height=1 node: chunk_1 + parent=cert_0. Validators verify chunk_1 is valid, verify parent cert_0 exists, then sign. The chain is now length 2. Gossip propagates cert_1.
visual_elements: Two node boxes chained vertically
Arrow from node 1 to cert_0 (its parent)
Validator verify checkmarks on both chunk and parent
Certificate cert_1 appearing below node 1

scenes.caption: TipAck combines a validator's acknowledgment with their current tip height. When gossiping, a validator shares both their vote and how far their chain has advanced. This allows other validators to detect gaps and request backfill.
visual_elements: Two TipAck packets showing ack + tip_height
Gap detection: validator_2's tip=3 means they are missing heights 4 and 5
Backfill request arrow from validator_1 to validator_2
Tooltip: 'tip_ack carries both the vote and the validator's current chain tip'

scenes.caption: The sequencer broadcasts two different chunks at height=2 with the same parent (cert_1). This is equivocation. Honest validators sign both — producing two different signatures from the same validator on the same height with different payloads. This is detectable as ChunkMismatch error.
system_events: ChunkMismatch detected: same validator, same height, different chunks
visual_elements: Fork visualization: two node boxes at height 2 with diverging chunk data
Same cert_1 parent pointing to both
validator_0 signature splitting and appearing on both branches
Error indicator: 'ChunkMismatch — equivocation detected'
Red highlight on validator_0 as the conflicting signer

scenes.caption: Epoch change: the validator set changes. The quorum for subsequent certificates uses the new N. The chain from the previous epoch continues, but the signature threshold is recalculated for the new validator set.
system_events: Validator set changes (2 validators leave, 1 joins)
New quorum threshold recalculated: N=3 → quorum=3
visual_elements: Epoch boundary line separating epoch 0 and 1
Old validator set (grayed) vs new validator set (active)
Quorum threshold updated
Chain continues through the boundary

checks: Each node's parent is a Certificate over the previous node — creates immutable chain
Equivocation at same height with same parent produces ChunkMismatch error
TipAck carries both the validator's acknowledgment and their current tip height for backfill
Certificate quorum = N3f1::quorum(N)
Epoch change resets the validator set and recalculates quorum threshold

visualizations.title: Marshal: From Broadcast to Application Delivery
visualizations.learning_goal: Trace how blocks flow from broadcast → simplex (notarization/finalization) → marshal actor → application, and understand where gaps trigger backfill.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Marshal is the bridge between consensus and the application layer. It is where ordered delivery guarantees are enforced, where gaps appear and trigger backfill, and where the floor/starting height determines which blocks the application receives.
visualizations.default_state: Standard mode, floor_height=0. Block stream at heights 1–8. No gaps yet. Simplex delivering notarizations and finalizations.

scenes.caption: In normal operation: broadcast delivers uncertified blocks → simplex delivers notarization/finalization certificates → marshal coordinates the two streams and delivers finalized blocks in height order.
system_events: simplex delivers notarization cert for view 3
simplex delivers finalization cert for view 3
visual_elements: Three-lane pipeline diagram: broadcast lane | simplex lane | marshal lane
Block flowing through broadcast lane (dashed border)
Notarization/finalization certificates in simplex lane
Marshal actor assembling both streams
Block 3 output arrow to 'Application'
Height counter incrementing: 1, 2, 3

scenes.caption: Marshal receives a finalization certificate for height=5 but does not have the block at height=4. This is a gap. Marshal pauses finalization of height 5 and emits a backfill request.
visual_elements: Pipeline diagram showing gap visualization
Block 5 certificate arriving but blocked (red pause icon)
Block 4 slot shown as empty/dashed box
BackfillRequest arrow pointing to resolver
Height 4 and 5 shown in red to indicate blocked state

scenes.caption: Resolver queries peers and receives block 4. Marshal verifies it matches the certified digest, then delivers blocks 4 and 5 in sequence. The pipeline resumes.
system_events: marshal verifies digest matches certified value
visual_elements: BackfillResponse arrow from resolver to marshal
Verify checkmark on block 4
Resume flow: blocks 4 and 5 released in order
Height counter: 4 → 5 (both delivered)
Pipeline resumes green

scenes.caption: In coding mode, marshal receives erasure-coded shards from coding::shards::Engine. Shards arrive from different peers. Marshal reconstructs the full block before issuing the finalization delivery.
visual_elements: Three shard boxes arriving in parallel from different peers
Shards labeled: 'shard_3_k_of_n'
Reconstruction engine box combining shards
Full block emerging from reconstruction
Marshal then treats as normal block for delivery

scenes.caption: When floor_height=5, marshal discards or ignores any block below height 5. This enables state sync from a snapshot rather than from genesis. Peers cannot backfill blocks below the floor.
visual_elements: Floor line drawn at height 5 in pipeline diagram
Blocks 1–4 shown crossing floor line but being dropped (X mark)
Blocks 5+ flowing normally
Caption: 'floor_height=5: blocks below this threshold are not stored or delivered'

checks: Marshal delivers blocks in monotonically increasing height — no gaps reported to application
When a finalization cert arrives but the prior block is missing, backfill is triggered
Backfill requests go to resolver, which queries peers
In coding mode, full block reconstruction via shards::Engine must succeed before delivery
Blocks below floor_height are ignored and not backfilled

visualizations.title: Domain Separation: Why _NOTARIZE ≠ _FINALIZE
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand how namespace suffixes prevent cross-protocol signature replay, and see exactly what bytes get signed under each subject.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Domain separation is a cryptographic discipline that prevents entire classes of attacks. A signature made under the notarize namespace cannot be replayed as a finalize signature — even if the underlying digest is identical. This makes cross-protocol attacks computationally infeasible.
visualizations.default_state: Proposal digest = 0xdeadbeef..., N=4, scheme=ed25519. Three namespace suffixes shown: _NOTARIZE, _NULLIFY, _FINALIZE.

scenes.caption: Every vote subject uses a distinct namespace suffix. The Namespace trait prepends the base _COMMONWARE_CONSENSUS_SIMPLEX namespace and appends the subject suffix. The derived namespace is passed to the cryptographic primitive.
visual_elements: Three namespace boxes stacked vertically
Base: _COMMONWARE_CONSENSUS_SIMPLEX
Notarize box: appends _NOTARIZE
Finalize box: appends _FINALIZE
Nullify box: appends _NULLIFY
Byte representation shown for each: _COMMONWARE_CONSENSUS_SIMPLEX_NOTARIZE

scenes.caption: validator_0 signs the proposal digest under ns_notarize. Anyone verifying under ns_notarize with validator_0's public key gets a valid result.
visual_elements: Digest box: 0xdeadbeef...
Signing operation: digest + namespace + secret_key → signature
Signature box (unique per signer and namespace)
Verify operation: signature + namespace + public_key → VALID (green checkmark)

scenes.caption: validator_0 signs under ns_notarize. An attacker tries to replay: they present sig_notarize to a verifier using ns_finalize. Verification fails — the namespace mismatch is detected.
system_events: verify(sig_notarize, digest=0xdeadbeef, namespace=ns_finalize, key=validator_0_pk) → INVALID (namespace mismatch)
visual_elements: Signature being fed into a different namespace verifier
Red X on verification result
Error label: 'namespace mismatch: expected _FINALIZE, got _NOTARIZE'
Caption: 'The signature bytes are valid, but the namespace binding is wrong'

scenes.caption: Nullify votes carry only a Round identifier (not a digest). The namespace suffix still applies: the same Round signed under _NULLIFY cannot be used as a notarize or finalize signature.
system_events: This signature cannot be replayed under ns_notarize or ns_finalize even with the same round
visual_elements: Round identifier (0, 3) as the payload (no digest)
Three namespace verification boxes
sig_nullify fails ns_notarize verification (red X)
sig_nullify fails ns_finalize verification (red X)
sig_nullify passes ns_nullify verification (green check)

checks: Namespace suffixes: _NOTARIZE, _NULLIFY, _FINALIZE are distinct byte sequences
sign(namespace=A, msg=M) ≠ sign(namespace=B, msg=M) for A ≠ B — different output
A signature created under namespace A cannot verify under namespace B
Nullify signs only Round (not a digest), still namespace-separated

visualizations.title: Parent Nullification: The Fork-Safety Gate
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand the parent nullification requirement: a validator cannot vote on a proposal whose parent view is behind their latest certified view, without first obtaining nullifications for the gap.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Without parent nullification enforcement, a malicious leader could publish a chain that skips an honest leader's block, and honest validators would unknowingly finalize it. This is the most subtle safety invariant in simplex.
visualizations.default_state: Validator V's latest certified view = 4 (has notarization certificate for view 4). Proposal arrives for view 6 with parent=(view=4, digest=...). Gap: view 5 is missing a nullification.

scenes.caption: The parent nullification check is: let V's latest certified view = L. Let proposal's parent view = P. If P < L, then V must hold nullifications (or notarizations) for ALL views in (P, L] before voting. This prevents skipping certified blocks.
visual_elements: Two-column layout: 'Validator V state' | 'Incoming Proposal'
V's latest certified view = 4 (notarization cert shown)
Proposal parent view = 4
Check formula: P < L? → 4 < 4? NO → vote allowed
Gap range (P+1 to L) shown as empty boxes between

scenes.caption: V's latest certified view = 4. Proposal at view 6 has parent view = 4. But view 5 nullification is missing. V cannot vote until they have view 5 nullification. The proposal is stored but not acted on.
system_events: check: gap views [5] need nullification — MISSING
voter stores proposal, waits for view 5 nullification
visual_elements: V's certified view marker at 4
Proposal arrow at view 6 pointing back to parent view 4
Gap indicator: view 5 shown as empty/missing
Voting blocked: red stop icon + 'waiting for view 5 nullification'
Proposal stored indicator

scenes.caption: View 5 nullification certificate arrives. Now V has all nullifications from P=4 to L=6 (view 5 nullify obtained). V can now vote on the view 6 proposal.
system_events: V updates latest known view to 5
recheck: P=4, L=5, no gap in (4, 5]
voter now sends Notarize vote for view 6 proposal
visual_elements: View 5 nullification cert appearing in V's state
Gap now closed (no empty boxes between parent and latest)
Recheck formula: P=4 < L=5? YES, but gap=[ ] (empty)
Voting unblocked: green checkmark + 'parent continuity satisfied'
Notarize vote arrow flowing out

scenes.caption: Attack scenario: Honest leader proposes block B at view 5. Validator V is slow and hasn't notarized it yet. Malicious leader at view 6 proposes a chain built on view 4 (skipping B). Without parent nullification enforcement, V would notarize the malicious chain — finalizing a chain that skips B. With enforcement, V requires view 5 nullification or notarization before voting.
system_events: honest_leader broadcasts proposal for view 5, block B
V is slow: has not received notarization for view 5
malicious_leader broadcasts proposal for view 6, parent=view_4 (skips B)
check: parent_view=4, latest_certified_view < 5? (V hasn't certified anything past 4)
without enforcement: V would notarize malicious chain
with enforcement: V requires view 5 nullification or notarization — not present — V blocks
visual_elements: Fork diagram: two chains diverging at view 4
Chain A (honest): view 4 → view 5 (B) → ... — shown in green
Chain B (malicious): view 4 → view 6 (skips B) — shown in red
V's state: stuck at view 4 certified
Malicious proposal blocked by 'parent continuity check FAILED'
Attack prevented label

checks: Parent nullification check: if parent_view < latest_certified_view, all intermediate views must have nullification certificates
Without parent nullification enforcement, a malicious leader can cause honest validators to finalize a chain that skips valid blocks
The check happens in voter actor before any notarize vote is sent
Nullification certificates for skipped views are required, not notarization certificates (can skip via nullify)
